perturbation.original_text:
6. Personal Data
6.1 Vendor shall not collect, use , process and/or disclose any Personal Data of ISCA’s clients or customers (collectively “ Customers ”) or of ISCA’s employees, without the prior written consent of ISCA.
6.2 Vendor shall at all times comply with the provisions of the PDPA and not cause ISCA t o be in breach of its obligations under the PDPA, including in relation to Personal Data of Customers and ISCA’s employees.
perturbation.changed_text:
11. General
11.1 No failure or delay by either Party in exercising any right under this Agreement shall be a waiver of such right.
11.2 No waiver of any obligation of this Ag reement shall be valid unless expressly made in writing.
11.3 If any part of this Agreement is, for any reason, declared invalid or unenforceable, the validity of the remaining part shall remain in full force and effect as if this Agreement had been executed wi thout the invalid part.
11.4 A person or entity who is not a Party to this Agreement shall have no right under the Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act (Cap. 53B) to enforce any term of this Agreement, regardless of whether such person or entity has been ide ntified by name, as a member of a class or as answering a particular description.
6. Personal Data
6.1 Vendor shall not collect, use , process and/or disclose any Personal Data of ISCA’s clients or customers (collectively “ Customers ”) or of ISCA’s employees, without the prior written consent of ISCA.
6.2 Vendor shall at all times comply with the provisions of the PDPA and not cause ISCA t o be in breach of its obligations under the PDPA, including in relation to Personal Data of Customers and ISCA’s employees.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the Personal Data clause to the 'General' section buries the important data protection obligations within boilerplate clauses, making it less prominent and potentially harder to enforce. Data protection requirements should be in their own explicit section.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 24 of the PDPA outlines the protection obligation, which requires organisations to protect personal data in their possession or control. By placing the vendor's obligations concerning personal data within a general section, the agreement obscures the specific and vital obligations to adhere to the PDPA, potentially weakening the enforcement of these data protection requirements. Section 6 previously was distinct and clear that it should not breach PDPA.

perturbation.justification: The original contract had a dedicated "Personal Data" section (Section 6) emphasizing data protection obligations. The changed text moves this section, burying it within a 'General' section. This placement obscures the specific and vital obligations to adhere to the PDPA as explained in the Law Explanation. The PDPC website excerpt emphasizes the importance of understanding and complying with the PDPA by providing resources and advisories. By making these obligations less prominent, the change potentially weakens the enforcement of these data protection requirements, thus creating a structural flaw that contradicts the intent of data protection laws.

perturbation.original_text: 9. Equitable Relief The Parties agree that in the event of any breach or threatened breach of this Agreement by the Vendor , ISCA shall be entitled to obtain equitable relief, including without any limitation, an injunction and/or specific performance, in addition to any other remedies available at law or in equity.
perturbation.changed_text:
5. Information Not Protected 5.1 The protection to be accorded to t he Confidential Information to be disclosed hereunder does not and shall not extend to any information which: (1) at the Effective Date is public knowledge or which subsequently becomes public knowledge other than by any breach of the terms of this Agreement; (2) is proven by Vendor by written evidence to have been already known to the Vendor at the Effective Date and not acquired directly or indirectly from ISCA , and such information has been obtained without restrictions on disclosure to others; or (3) is required to be disclosed by applicable law or order of a court of competent jurisdiction or recognised stock exchange or government department or agency with valid authority, provided that prior to such disclosure the Vendor consults with ISCA as to the proposed form , scope, nature, and purpose of the disclosure, co -operates with ISCA to limit the extent of the disclosure, and permits ISCA time to obtain legal remedies to maintain such Confidential Information in confidence.
9. Equitable Relief The Parties agree that in the event of any breach or threatened breach of this Agreement by the Vendor , ISCA shall be entitled to obtain equitable relief, including without any limitation, an injunction and/or specific performance, in addition to any other remedies available at law or in equity.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the Equitable Relief clause to the 'Information Not Protected' section, it appears that seeking legal recourse for breach is only applicable when confidential information is at risk. This is misleading, as equitable relief should be available for any breach of the agreement, not just breaches involving confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Specific Relief Act provides for remedies such as specific performance and injunctions for breach of contract. By placing the equitable relief clause under 'Information Not Protected,' the contract implies that such relief is only available when confidential information is involved, which is a misrepresentation of the full scope of remedies available for contractual breaches under Singapore law. In contract law, remedies are not exclusively for breaches of confidentially, and rather for any breach of contract. 9 was distinct before now.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Ch. 08 The Law of Contract www.singaporelawwatch.sg COMMERCIAL LAW Search Ch. 08 The Law of Contract Ch. 08A Remedies for Breach of Contract Ch. 09 Domestic Sale of Goods Ch. 10 International Sale of Goods Ch. 11 The Law of Credit and Security Ch. 12 Intellectual Property Law Ch. 13 Intellectual Property Licensing Ch. 14 Forms of Business Organisations Ch. 15 Law of Agency Ch. 16 Singapore Company Law Ch. 17 Corporate Finance and Securities Regulation Ch. 18 Equity and Trusts Ch. 19 Restitution Ch. 20 The Law of Negligence Ch. 21 Economic Torts Ch. 22 Banking and Finance Ch. 23 The Law of Guarantees Ch. 24 Insurance Law Ch. 25 Shipping Law Ch. 26 Building and Construction Law Ch. 27 Competition Law Ch. 28 Income Taxation Ch. 29 Land Law Ch. 30 Bankruptcy and Insolvency Ch. 31 Technology, Media, and Telecom No content A problem occurred while loading content. Previous Next Ch. 08 The Law of Contract SECTION 1 GENERAL APPLICATION A. Singapore contract law largely based on English contract law 8.1.1 Contract law in Singapore is largely based on the common law of contract in England. Hence, the rules developed in the Singapore courts do bear a very close resemblance to those developed under English common law. Indeed, where there is no Singapore authority specifically on point, it will usually be assumed that the position will, in the first instance, be no different from that in England. Unlike its neighbours Malaysia and Brunei, following Independence in 1965, Singapore’s Parliament made no attempt to codify Singapore’s law of contract. Accordingly, much of the law of contract in Singapore remains in the form of judge-made rules. B. Certain aspects of Singapore contract law based on specific statutes 8.1.2 In some circumstances, these judge-made rules have been modified by specific statutes. Many of these statutes are English in origin. To begin with, 13 English commercial statutes have been incorporated as part of the Statutes of the Republic of Singapore by virtue of s 4 of the Application of English Law Act (Cap 7A, 1993 Rev Ed). These are listed in Part II of the First Schedule of this Act. Other statutes, eg the Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act (Cap 53B, 2002 Rev Ed), are modelled upon English statutes. There are also other areas where statutory development based on non-English models has taken place, eg the Consumer Protection (Fair Trading) Act (Cap 52A, 2004 Rev Ed) (which was largely drawn from fair trading legislation enacted in Alberta and Sasketchewan). SECTION 2 OFFER AND ACCEPTANCE A. Contract is essentially agreement between two or more parties 8.2.1 A contract is essentially an agreement between two or more parties, the terms of which affect their respective rights and obligations which are enforceable at law. Whether the parties have reached agreement, or a meeting of the minds, is objectively ascertained from the facts. The concepts of offer and acceptance provide in many, albeit not all, cases the starting point for analysing whether agreement has been reached. B. Offer 1. Definition of offer 8.2.2 An offer is a promise, or other expression of willingness, by the ‘offeror’ to be bound on certain specified terms upon the unqualified acceptance of these terms by the person to whom the offer is made (the ‘offeree’). Provided the other formation elements (ie consideration and intention to create legal relations) are present, the acceptance of an offer results in a valid contract. 2. Offer must be made with objectively ascertained intention to be bound 8.2.3 Whether any particular statement amounts to an offer depends on the intention with which it is made. An offer must be made with the intention to be bound. On the other hand, if a person is merely soliciting offers or requesting for information, without any intention to be bound, at best, he or she would be making an invitation to treat. Under the objective test, a person may be said to have made an offer if his or her statement (or conduct) induces a reas
perturbation.justification: The changed text, by placing the equitable relief clause under the 'Information Not Protected' section, misrepresents the scope of remedies available for contractual breaches. The 'Law Explanation' states that remedies are not exclusively for breaches of confidentiality, but rather for any breach of contract. Snippet 2 speaks to remedies for breach of contract in Singapore, implying there are options beyond confidentiality breaches.

perturbation.original_text:
10. Governing Law
10.1 This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accord ance with Singapore law.
10.2 The Parties hereby submit to the jurisdiction of the Singapore courts.
perturbation.changed_text:
3. Employee Undertakings
3.1 The Vendor shall, before any permitted disclosures are made to any employees pursuant to Clause 2.2(1) , inform such employees of the Vendor ’s obligations under this Agreement and bind such employees to observe the terms of this Agreement.
3.2 For the avoidance of doubt, the Vendor shall be responsible for ensuring its employees’ strict compliance with the terms of this Agreement. Any non -compliance with the terms of this Agreement by an employee of the Vendor shall be deemed a breach of this Agreement by Vendor .
10. Governing Law
10.1 This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accord ance with Singapore law.
10.2 The Parties hereby submit to the jurisdiction of the Singapore courts.
perturbation.explanation: Placing the 'Governing Law' clause under 'Employee Undertakings' obscures the overall legal framework of the agreement. It incorrectly suggests that Singapore law only applies to employee-related aspects of the NDA, instead of governing the entire contract. It also does not logically fit under the employee section.
perturbation.law_citation: Choice of Law Principles in Singapore
perturbation.law_explanation: Singapore contract law recognizes the importance of a clear choice of law clause for the entire agreement, not just a specific section. Moving this clause into the 'Employee Undertakings' section implies that Singapore law only governs matters related to employees, which is a misleading and incorrect representation of how governing law clauses operate and what is expected under Singapore contract law. It is also expected to govern the entire contract.

perturbation.justification: The relocation of the 'Governing Law' clause within the contract, specifically under 'Employee Undertakings,' creates a structural flaw that leads to a legal contradiction. This is because it obscures the overall legal framework intended to govern the entire agreement, not just the employee-related aspects. As per the Perturbation Explanation, it incorrectly suggests Singapore law only applies to employee-related aspects. Although the scraped snippets don't explicitly address governing law placement, the Law Explanation argues that Singapore contract law recognizes the need for a clear choice of law for the entire agreement. Placing it within a specific section is a misleading representation of how governing law clauses should function. The structural flaw, according to the Perturbation Definition, arises when a required legal disclosure is placed in a misleading section, making it difficult to interpret, thus creating a contradiction.
